title: Apollo 11 stones
description: Found in the Huns Mountains of Namibia. These are some of the oldest examples of representational art in Africa.

title: Great Hall of the Bulls
description: A massive cave complex featuring composite view renderings of bulls, horses, and deer.

title: Camelid sacrum in the shape of a canine
description: The sacrum symbolized the soul and fertility in Mesoamerican culture.

title: Running horned woman
description: Features a female figure with horns, likely a goddess or priestess.

title: Beaker with ibex motifs
description: A funerary object featuring stylized animals with oversized geometric features.

title: Anthropomorphic stele
description: A grave marker reflecting early regional trade and stylistic exchange.

title: Jade cong
description: A ritual object with a square exterior (earth) and circular interior (sky).

title: Stonehenge
description: A megalithic monument built in phases, used for astronomical and ritual purposes.

title: The Ambum Stone
description: One of the oldest sculptures in Oceania, possibly a ritual pestle.

title: Tlatilco female figurine
description: Small ceramic figures often depicting women with two faces, symbolizing duality.

title: Terra cotta fragment
description: Features the oldest known human face in Oceanic art from the Lapita culture.

title: White Temple and its ziggurat
description: A high platform built to bring priests closer to the god Anu.

title: Palette of King Narmer
description: Commemorates the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt.

title: Statues of votive figures
description: Figures with wide eyes, signifying eternal wakefulness and devotion.

title: Seated scribe
description: A rare non-idealized portrait denoting literacy and wealth.

title: Standard of Ur
materials: Wood inlaid with shell, lapis lazuli, and red limestone
description: A hollow wooden box with two main panels: War and Peace. It uses hierarchical scale to show the importance of the leader.

title: Great Pyramids and Great Sphinx
description: Consists of three pyramids (Khufu, Khafre, Menkaure). The Sphinx is carved from live rock and represents the pharaoh.

title: King Menkaura and queen
description: Features the pharaoh and his queen in a rigid, frontal pose, symbolizing eternal power and stability.

title: The Code of Hammurabi
description: A stele inscribed with 282 laws. The top shows Hammurabi receiving the laws from the sun god Shamash.

title: Temple of Amun-Re and Hypostyle Hall
date: Temple: c. 1550 B.C.E.; Hall: c. 1250 B.C.E.
description: A massive religious complex featuring clerestory lighting to allow air and light into the dark hall.

title: Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut
materials: Sandstone, partially carved into rock cliff
description: Designed by Senmut for the female pharaoh Hatshepsut. It features three terraces connected by ramps.

title: Akhenaton, Nefertiti, and three daughters
description: Depicts the royal family in an informal, intimate setting under the sun disk Aten. A radical stylistic shift from traditional Egyptian art.

title: Tutankhamun's tomb, innermost coffin
materials: Gold with inlay of enamel and semiprecious stones
description: The boy king is shown as Osiris, holding a crook and flail. Made of 143kg of solid gold.

title: Last judgment of Hu-Nefer
description: From a Book of the Dead. It shows the god Anubis weighing Hu-Nefer’s heart against a feather.

title: Lamassu from the citadel of Sargon II
description: Colossal winged, human-headed bulls with five legs, designed to look stationary from the front and moving from the side.

title: Athenian agora
description: The central public space in ancient Athens, serving as a marketplace and a center for democracy and philosophy.

title: Anavysos Kouros
description: A grave marker for a fallen soldier (Kroisos). Shows the 'archaic smile' and a move toward more naturalistic anatomy.

title: Peplos Kore from the Acropolis
description: A votive statue of a young woman. Originally brightly painted; she may be a goddess rather than a mortal.

title: Sarcophagus of the Spouses
description: A funerary container showing a husband and wife reclining together, reflecting the high status of women in Etruscan society.

title: Audience Hall (apadana) of Darius and Xerxes
description: A massive ceremonial hall used to receive guests. Reliefs show diverse peoples bringing tribute to the Persian king.

title: Temple of Minerva and sculpture of Apollo
description: An Etruscan temple with a high podium and frontal entrance. The Apollo of Veii was placed on the roof, showing dynamic movement and Etruscan style.

title: Tomb of the Triclinium
description: A subterranean burial chamber featuring frescoes of people banqueting, dancing, and playing music, celebrating the life of the deceased.

title: Niobides Krater
description: Depicts the slaughter of Niobe's children by Apollo and Artemis. Notable for showing figures on different levels, indicating an early attempt at spatial depth.

title: Doryphoros (Spear Bearer)
materials: Roman marble copy of a Greek bronze original
description: Created to illustrate Polykleitos's 'Canon' of proportions. It perfectly demonstrates the 'contrapposto' pose and idealized human form.

title: Acropolis (Parthenon, Temple of Athena Nike)
description: The peak of Greek architecture. Built under Pericles, it represents the victory of democracy and civilization over 'barbarians'.

title: Grave stele of Hegeso
description: A domestic scene on a grave marker showing Hegeso looking at jewelry with her servant. Reflects the private life of Athenian women.

title: Winged Victory of Samothrace
description: Nike (Victory) landing on a ship's prow. Noted for its dramatic sense of motion, swirling drapery, and emotional intensity.

title: Great Altar of Zeus and Athena at Pergamon
description: The Gigantomachy frieze shows the gods fighting giants. It’s the ultimate example of Hellenistic drama, diagonal lines, and high relief.

title: House of the Vettii
date: c. second century B.C.E.; rebuilt c. 62–79 C.E.
description: A Roman domus featuring an atrium and peristyle garden. Famous for its Fourth Style frescoes and showing the status of freedmen.

title: Alexander Mosaic from the House of the Faun
artist: Republican Roman (copy of a Greek wall painting)
description: Depicts Alexander the Great defeating the Persian King Darius III at the Battle of Issus. Uses foreshortening and modeling to create drama.

title: Seated boxer
description: Unlike idealized Classical statues, this shows an aging, battered athlete. Emphasizes suffering, exhaustion, and human emotion.

title: Head of a Roman patrician
description: A 'veristic' (hyper-realistic) portrait. The wrinkles and age represent gravitas, wisdom, and service to the state.

title: Augustus of Prima Porta
description: Political propaganda showing Augustus as an eternal, god-like leader. Uses the Doryphoros's pose but with Roman distinctness.

title: Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheater)
description: Built using concrete arches and vaults. The exterior features Tuscan, Ionic, and Corinthian columns in ascending order.

title: Forum of Trajan
description: A massive civic complex including a basilica, markets, and the Column of Trajan which narrates his victory over the Dacians.

title: Pantheon
description: A temple to all gods, featuring a massive dome with a central oculus. The coffered ceiling lightens the weight of the concrete structure.

title: Ludovisi Battle Sarcophagus
description: Depicts a battle between Romans and Goths. The crowded, chaotic composition marks a shift away from Classical calm toward emotional intensity.

title: Catacomb of Priscilla
description: An underground burial site containing early Christian iconography, such as the Good Shepherd and the Orant figure.

title: Santa Sabina
description: An early Christian basilica with a simple exterior and a light-filled interior, symbolizing the spiritual purity of the soul.

title: Rebecca and Eliezer at the Well and Jacob Wrestling the Angel
description: One of the oldest surviving illustrated biblical manuscripts. Uses silver ink on purple-dyed vellum, denoting royal patronage.

title: San Vitale
materials: Brick, marble, and stone veneer; mosaic
description: A centrally planned octagonal church famous for its mosaics of Emperor Justinian and Empress Theodora.

title: Hagia Sophia
artist: Anthemius of Tralles and Isidorus of Miletus
materials: Brick and stone veneer and mosaic
description: The masterpiece of Byzantine architecture. Its dome appears to float on a 'golden chain from heaven' thanks to the use of pendentives.

title: Merovingian looped fibulae
materials: Silver gilt worked in filigree, with inlays of garnet and other stones
description: Clasps used to fasten garments. Features 'cloisonné' technique and zoomorphic elements like eagles and fish.

title: Virgin (Theotokos) and Child between Saints Theodore and George
date: Sixth or early seventh century C.E.
description: A rare icon that survived the Iconoclasm. The saints look at the viewer, while the Virgin looks away, creating a hierarchy of holiness.

title: Lindisfarne Gospels
materials: Illuminated manuscript (ink, pigments, and gold on vellum)
description: Hiberno-Saxon art blending Celtic knots, interlacing animal patterns, and Christian themes.

title: Great Mosque of Cordoba
description: Famous for its hypostyle hall with red-and-white striped double-arched columns and its ornate mihrab.

title: Pyxis of al-Mughira
description: An ivory container for aromatics. Features intricate carvings of courtly life and animals, used as a gift for a prince.

title: Church of Sainte-Foy
description: A pilgrimage church featuring a tympanum of the Last Judgment. Houses the reliquary of the young martyr Saint Foy.

title: Bayeux Tapestry
description: A 70-meter long narrative work depicting the Norman Conquest of England and the Battle of Hastings.

title: Chartres Cathedral
date: Original construction c. 1145–1155 C.E.; reconstructed c. 1194–1220 C.E.
description: The quintessential Gothic cathedral. Known for its stained glass, flying buttresses, and the tunic of the Virgin Mary.

title: Dedication Page with Blanche of Castile and King Louis IX of France
description: From a Moralized Bible. Shows the queen mother teaching her son, reflecting the link between divine and royal power.

title: Röttgen Pietà
description: A devotional image (Andachtsbild) showing Mary mourning Jesus. Emphasizes suffering and human emotion to provoke empathy.

title: Arena (Scrovegni) Chapel
description: Giotto's frescoes represent a transition to the Renaissance, featuring realistic space and deep emotional expression.

title: Golden Haggadah
description: A Jewish text used to narrate the Passover. The style is influenced by French Gothic courtly art.

title: Alhambra
materials: Whitewashed adobe stucco, wood, tile, paint, and gilding
description: A palace and fortress complex. Known for its intricate 'muqarnas' carvings and the Court of the Lions.

title: Annunciation Triptych (Mérode Altarpiece)
description: A Northern Renaissance altarpiece filled with 'disguised symbolism' in a domestic setting.

title: Pazzi Chapel
description: A perfect example of Early Renaissance rationality, using 'pietra serena' (gray stone) to highlight geometric harmony.

title: The Arnolfini Portrait
description: A masterwork of Northern Renaissance realism. Famous for the convex mirror and the detailed use of oil paint.

title: David
description: The first free-standing nude statue since antiquity. Represents Florence's identity through the biblical hero.

title: Palazzo Rucellai
description: Uses Classical orders (Tuscan, Ionic, Corinthian) on the facade, reflecting the humanist recovery of ancient Roman style.

title: Madonna and Child with Two Angels
description: Shows a more human and playful depiction of the Virgin Mary, using his own lover as the model.

title: Birth of Venus
description: Inspired by Neoplatonist philosophy. It's the first large-scale painting on canvas in Florence, showing a pagan goddess nude.

title: Last Supper
description: Uses linear perspective to center on Jesus. Leonardo experimented with paint layers, causing the work to deteriorate early.

title: Adam and Eve
description: Combines Italian Renaissance proportions with Northern Renaissance attention to naturalistic detail and symbolism.

title: Sistine Chapel ceiling and Altar wall frescoes
description: The ceiling tells the story of Genesis; the Last Judgment on the altar wall shows a later, more Mannerist and turbulent style.

title: School of Athens
description: Commissioned by Pope Julius II for his library. It celebrates human reason and the classical heritage, with Plato and Aristotle at the center of a perfectly balanced composition.

title: Isenheim altarpiece
description: Placed in a hospital for skin diseases. The gruesome depiction of Christ's suffering was meant to comfort patients by showing that God shared their pain.

title: Entombment of Christ
description: A prime example of Mannerism. Features elongated limbs, an ambiguous setting, and jarring colors, moving away from the stability of the High Renaissance.

title: Allegory of Law and Grace
description: The most influential graphic work of the Protestant Reformation. It contrasts the 'Old Law' (leading to hell) with the 'New Grace' (leading to salvation through faith).

title: Venus of Urbino
description: Established the 'reclining female nude' as a major genre. Noted for its sensuality, 'glazing' technique for soft skin tones, and rich Venetian color.

title: Frontispiece of the Codex Mendoza
description: Documents the history and tribute system of the Aztec Empire for Spanish royalty. Shows the eagle on a cactus, the founding myth of Tenochtitlan.

title: Il Gesù, including Triumph of the Name of Jesus fresco
artist: Giacomo da Vignola (architect); Giacomo della Porta (facade); Giovanni Battista Gaulli (fresco)
description: The mother church of the Jesuits. The ceiling fresco uses 'trompe l'oeil' to blur the line between the physical architecture and the heavenly sky.

title: Hunters in the Snow
description: A genre painting showing the labors of the months. It captures the vastness of nature and the daily lives of peasants in a world gripped by winter.

title: Mosque of Selim II
description: Sinan's masterpiece, intended to surpass the Hagia Sophia. Features an enormous dome supported by eight massive piers and four slender minarets.

title: Calling of Saint Matthew
description: Uses extreme 'tenebrism' (light and shadow). The divine light follows Jesus' hand, calling a tax collector to a spiritual life in a gritty, realistic setting.

title: Henri IV Receives the Portrait of Marie de' Medici
description: Part of a cycle of 24 paintings. Combines historical figures with mythological deities (like Cupid and Hymen) to glorify Marie de' Medici's marriage.

title: Self-Portrait with Saskia
description: Rembrandt's only etching of himself with his wife. He presents himself as an artist at work, using spontaneous, sketch-like lines.

title: San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane
description: The quintessential Baroque church. Features a pulsating, wave-like facade and a complex oval dome, rejecting the static geometry of the Renaissance.

title: Ecstasy of Saint Teresa
description: A multisensory Counter-Reformation masterpiece. Uses hidden light and theatrical bronze rays to depict the saint's spiritual union with God.

title: Angel with Arquebus, Asiel Timor Dei
description: Represents the 'celestial army' of the Spanish colonies. The angel wears Andean aristocratic clothing and carries a gun, symbolizing power and protection.

title: Las Meninas
description: A complex painting about the act of painting itself. Features the Infanta Margaret Theresa, Velázquez himself, and the king and queen in a mirror.

title: Woman Holding a Balance
description: A quiet Dutch genre scene. The balance and the 'Last Judgment' painting in the background suggest a life of moderation and spiritual self-reflection.

title: The Palace at Versailles
artist: Louis Le Vau and Jules Hardouin-Mansart (architects)
materials: Masonry, stone, wood, iron, and gold leaf; gardens
description: The symbol of absolute monarchy. The Hall of Mirrors and vast gardens were designed to showcase Louis XIV, the 'Sun King', as the center of the universe.

title: Screen with the Siege of Belgrade and hunting scene
materials: Tempera and resin on wood, shell inlay
description: A 'biombo' showing global exchange. It features a European battle on one side and a Japanese-inspired landscape on the other, made with Mexican materials.

title: The Virgin of Guadalupe
materials: Oil on wood on canvas, inlaid with mother-of-pearl
description: Created using the 'enconchado' (shell-inlay) technique. It depicts the patron saint of Mexico, blending Catholic and indigenous identities.

title: Fruit and Insects
description: A Dutch 'still life' or 'vanitas' painting. The decaying fruit and insects remind the viewer of the passage of time and the fragility of life.

title: Spaniard and Indian Produce a Mestizo
description: Part of a 'Casta painting' series. These were used to categorize and document the complex racial hierarchy in the Spanish Americas.

title: The Tête à Tête, from Marriage à la Mode
description: A satirical series critiquing the upper class. It shows a newly married couple who have already descended into immorality and financial ruin.

title: Portrait of Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz
description: Depicts a famous Mexican nun and intellectual. She is shown in her library, asserting her status as a scholar in a male-dominated society.

title: A Philosopher Giving a Lecture on the Orrery
description: Reflects the Enlightenment's fascination with science. Uses the 'tenebrism' of religious art to sanctify a scientific demonstration of the solar system.

title: The Swing
description: A masterpiece of Rococo. Features pastels, soft light, and a playful, flirtatious scene of the French aristocracy at leisure.

title: Monticello
description: Jefferson's self-designed home. It brings the Neoclassical ideals of democracy and rationality to the American landscape.

title: The Oath of the Horatii
description: The quintessential Neoclassical painting. It champions civic duty and patriotism over family, setting the tone for the French Revolution.

title: George Washington
description: Shows Washington not as a king, but as a modern Cincinnatus—a gentleman farmer who laid down his sword for his country.

title: Self-Portrait
description: A late Rococo/Neoclassical hybrid. The artist depicts herself confidently at work on a portrait of Marie Antoinette, asserting her professional identity.

title: And there’s nothing to be done (Y no hai remedio)
description: Part of 'The Disasters of War' series. A brutal critique of the French occupation of Spain, showing the dehumanizing effects of war and execution.

title: La Grande Odalisque
description: A bridge between Neoclassicism and Romanticism. Ingres distorted anatomy (adding extra vertebrae) to prioritize the sensuality and graceful lines of the figure.

title: Liberty Leading the People
description: Commemorates the July Revolution of 1830. Liberty is personified as a woman leading people of all social classes over the barricades.

title: The Oxbow
description: Hudson River School masterpiece. It contrasts wild, untamed nature with cultivated, settled land, reflecting the idea of Manifest Destiny.

title: Still Life in Studio
description: One of the earliest successful photographs. It mimicked traditional still-life paintings to argue that photography should be considered a fine art.

title: Slave Ship (Slavers Throwing Overboard the Dead and Dying, Typhoon Coming On)
description: Romanticism at its most intense. Turner uses expressive color and brushwork to show the sublime power of the sea and the cruelty of the slave trade.

title: Palace of Westminster (Houses of Parliament)
artist: Charles Barry and Augustus W. N. Pugin (Architects)
description: A Gothic Revival masterpiece. It rejected the industrial 'ugly' present in favor of a nationalistic, medieval past.

title: The Stone Breakers
description: Realism. Courbet depicted the grueling, low-status labor of the poor on a scale usually reserved for 'important' history paintings.

title: Nadar Raising Photography to the Height of Art
description: A satirical print mocking the photographer Nadar and the legal debate over whether photography was art or merely a mechanical process.

title: Olympia
description: Manet shocked Paris by painting a prostitute who looks directly at the viewer with a defiant gaze, stripping away the 'mythological' veil of the nude.

title: The Saint-Lazare Station
description: Impressionism. Monet focuses on the play of light and steam in the modern industrial environment, rather than the train itself.

title: The Horse in Motion
description: Used stop-motion photography to prove that all four of a horse's hooves leave the ground during a gallop. A precursor to cinema.

title: The Valley of Mexico from the Hillside of Santa Isabel
description: Celebrates Mexican national identity by blending Romantic landscape techniques with specific local geography and history.

title: The Burghers of Calais
description: Instead of a heroic monument, Rodin shows the citizens as miserable, weary individuals facing death, placed at ground level for the viewer.

title: The Starry Night
description: Post-Impressionism. Van Gogh uses expressive, swirling brushstrokes to convey his emotional state while in an asylum.

title: The Coiffure
description: Influenced by Japanese woodblock prints (Japonisme). It shows a private, domestic moment with simplified lines and flat color.

title: The Scream
description: Symbolism/Expressionism. Captures an internal scream of anxiety. The landscape and sky vibrate with the character's emotion.

title: Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?
description: A massive philosophical work intended as a 'final testament.' Blends Tahitian subjects with a Christian-style fresco composition.

title: Carson, Pirie, Scott and Company Building
materials: Iron, steel, glass, and terra cotta
description: Sullivan’s 'form follows function.' The building highlights its steel frame with a grid-like exterior and ornate ironwork at the base.

title: Mont Sainte-Victoire
description: Cézanne deconstructed nature into geometric shapes and planes of color, paving the way for Cubism.

title: Les Demoiselles d’Avignon
description: Foundational work of Modernism. Picasso broke the human body into jagged shards, influenced by African masks and Iberian sculpture.

title: The Steerage
description: A turning point in photography toward Modernism. Stieglitz focused on the formal arrangement of shapes and lines (the bridge, the ladder) rather than just the story.

title: The Kiss
materials: Oil and gold leaf on canvas
description: Art Nouveau/Vienna Secession. Uses decorative patterns and heavy gold leaf to create a transcendent, almost Byzantine-style icon of love.

title: The Kiss
description: Abstract sculpture. Brancusi carved directly into the block, reducing the figures to their most essential geometric forms.

title: The Portuguese
description: Analytical Cubism. Braque fragmented the subject (a guitar player) so much that it is almost unrecognizable, forcing the viewer to look at form and letters.

title: Goldfish
description: Fauvism. Matisse uses vibrant, non-naturalistic color to create a sense of harmony and peace, reflecting his trip to Morocco.

title: Improvisation 28 (second version)
description: One of the first truly abstract paintings. Kandinsky believed color and line could communicate spiritual truths like music (synesthesia).

title: Self-Portrait as a Soldier
description: German Expressionism (Die Brücke). Captures the psychological trauma of WWI, symbolized by his phantom 'severed hand'.

title: Memorial Sheet for Karl Liebknecht
description: A lamentation-style print for a murdered communist leader. The woodcut medium adds a raw, emotional intensity to the mourning crowd.

title: Villa Savoye
description: International Style. Demonstrates Le Corbusier's 'Five Points of Architecture,' including pilotis, a ribbon window, and a roof garden.

title: Composition with Red, Blue and Yellow
description: A prime example of De Stijl and Neo-Plasticism. Mondrian used primary colors and asymmetrical grids to express a universal harmonic order.

title: Illustration from The Results of the First Five-Year Plan
description: Soviet Constructivist propaganda using scale, high-contrast photography, and the color red to symbolize the success of industrialization.

title: Object (Le Déjeuner en fourrure)
description: A Surrealist masterpiece that provokes visceral sensory discomfort by merging a domestic object with the primal texture of fur.

title: Fallingwater
materials: Reinforced concrete, sandstone, steel, and glass
description: The pinnacle of organic architecture, cantilevered directly over a waterfall to harmonize the structure with its natural environment.

title: The Two Fridas
description: A dual self-portrait representing Kahlo's split identity (European and Mexican) and the emotional pain of her divorce from Diego Rivera.

title: The Migration of the Negro, Panel no. 49
description: Part of a 60-panel series depicting the Great Migration. This panel captures the segregation and public tension in the North.

title: The Jungle
materials: Gouache on paper mounted on canvas
description: A blend of Surrealism, Cubism, and Afro-Caribbean Santería culture, critiquing the history of the sugar industry in Cuba.

title: Dream of a Sunday Afternoon in the Alameda Park
description: A panoramic mural depicting 400 years of Mexican history, featuring historical figures, the artist, and the symbol of La Catrina.

title: Fountain
materials: Ready-made glazed sanitary china with black paint
description: The most famous 'readymade' in art history, Duchamp challenged the definition of art by selecting a mundane object and recontextualizing it.

title: Woman, I
description: Abstract Expressionism. De Kooning used aggressive, layered brushwork to explore the female figure as a primal and menacing archetype.

title: Seagram Building
artist: Ludwig Mies van der Rohe and Philip Johnson
materials: Steel frame with glass curtain wall and bronze
description: A masterpiece of the International Style skyscraper, emphasizing 'less is more' with its symmetrical bronze-clad exterior.

title: Marilyn Diptych
materials: Oil, acrylic, and silkscreen enamel on canvas
description: Pop Art. Warhol uses mass-production techniques to comment on the celebrity cult and the commodification of Marilyn Monroe's image.

title: Narcissus Garden
description: An anti-market environmental piece composed of 1,500 mirrored spheres, intended to reflect the vanity of the viewer and the art world.

title: The Bay
description: Color Field painting. Frankenthaler used the 'soak-stain' technique, pouring diluted paint onto unprimed canvas to emphasize color's flat quality.

title: Lipstick (Ascending) on Caterpillar Tracks
materials: Cor-Ten steel, steel, aluminum, and cast resin
description: A Pop Art monument contrasting consumerism (lipstick) with the military (tank tracks) as a protest against the Vietnam War.

title: Spiral Jetty
materials: Earthwork: mud, salt crystals, rocks, and water
description: A massive 1,500-foot coil in the Great Salt Lake that explores the relationship between art, nature, and the concept of entropy.

title: House in New Castle County
artist: Robert Venturi, John Rauch, and Denise Scott Brown
description: A Postmodern landmark. It rejects Modernism's purity by embracing historical references, decoration, and whimsical silhouettes.

title: Chavín de Huántar
description: A major Andean religious center where pilgrims underwent shamanistic transformation, marked by the fanged Lanzón Stela.

title: Mesa Verde cliff dwellings
description: A massive communal living site built into natural alcoves, featuring circular kivas used for spiritual and community gathering.

title: Yaxchilán
description: A Maya city known for its intricate lintels, such as Lintel 25, which depicts Lady Xoc performing a bloodletting vision ritual.

title: Great Serpent Mound
description: A 1,300-foot-long snake effigy likely used for marking solar events like the summer solstice or representing a serpent deity.

title: Templo Mayor (Main Temple)
description: The religious heart of the Aztec Empire, featuring twin temples dedicated to the gods of war (Huitzilopochtli) and rain (Tlaloc).

title: Ruler's feather headdress (probably Motecuhzoma II)
description: A symbol of divine kingship and status, made from hundreds of rare iridescent quetzal tail feathers.

title: City of Cusco
description: The 'Navel of the World' for the Inka. Built in the shape of a puma, it features the sacred Qorikancha (Temple of the Sun).

title: Maize cobs
description: Found in the Garden of the Qorikancha, these realistic metal plants celebrated the Inka's control over nature and agricultural wealth.

title: City of Machu Picchu
description: A royal estate for Pachacuti, showcasing masterful stone masonry that integrates the architecture with the mountain peaks.

title: All-T'oqapu tunic
description: A high-status garment worn by the Sapa Inka. Its variety of geometric motifs (t'oqapu) symbolize the unity of the entire empire.

title: Bandolier bag
description: Adapted from European ammunition pouches, these bags became prestige items featuring complex, symbolic floral patterns in glass beads.

title: Transformation mask
description: Worn in Potlatch ceremonies. The mask pulls open to reveal a human face inside an animal, symbolizing the mythic transformation of ancestors.

title: Painted elk hide
description: Created for the tourist trade, it preserves memories of traditional Shoshone life, including the Sun Dance and buffalo hunts.

title: Black-on-black ceramic vessel
description: A revival of ancient Puebloan pottery techniques. Maria created the form, while Julian painted the matte designs, transforming a utilitarian object into fine art.

title: Great Zimbabwe (Circular Wall & Conical Tower)
description: A royal city and trade hub. The conical tower and massive dry-stone walls symbolize the king's power and were built without mortar.

title: Great Mosque of Djenné
description: The largest mud-brick building in the world. It requires annual communal maintenance (Crepissage) to protect its facade from the rainy season.

title: Wall plaque, from Oba’s palace
description: Originally part of a series decorating the royal palace. It uses hierarchical scale to emphasize the Oba (king) as the central authority.

title: Sika dwa kofi (Golden Stool)
materials: Gold over wood and cast-gold attachments
description: The most sacred object of the Asante nation, believed to contain the soul of the people. It is never allowed to touch the ground.

title: Ndop (portrait figure) of King Mishe miShyaang maMbul
description: An idealized commemorative portrait of a king. Each Ndop is identified by a specific symbol (ibol); for this king, it is a drum.

title: Power figure (Nkisi n’kondi)
description: A spiritual container used to seal oaths or settle disputes. Nails or blades are driven into the wood to 'awaken' the spirit within.

title: Female (Pwo) mask
description: Worn by men in dances that honor fertile women. The mask's calm features and tattoos represent the ideal of maternal beauty and wisdom.

title: Portrait mask (Mblo)
description: Part of a public performance (Gbagba) used to honor a respected member of society, reflecting their inner beauty and character.

title: Bundu mask
description: The only mask in Africa worn exclusively by women. It is used during girls' initiation ceremonies to teach them the ideals of womanhood.

title: Ikenga (shrine figure)
description: A personal shrine representing a man's achievement and his 'right hand.' The prominent horns symbolize strength and determination.

title: Lukasa (memory board)
description: A mnemonic tool used by court historians to recount Luba history, genealogy, and the laws of the kingdom through tactile beads.

title: Aka elephant mask
materials: Wood, woven cloth, reeds, and beads
description: Worn by the elite Kuosi society in dances that celebrate the Fon's (king's) power, combining elephant and leopard motifs.

title: Reliquary figure (nsek-bieri)
description: Designed to sit on boxes containing ancestor bones. The figure’s balanced, calm posture acts as a guardian for the sacred remains.

title: Veranda post of enthroned king and senior wife (Opo Ogoga)
description: A masterpiece of Yoruba carving. The senior wife's larger scale highlights her essential role in crowning and supporting the king.

title: Petra (Treasury and Great Temple)
description: A rock-cut city that served as a capital and major trade crossroad, blending Hellenistic architecture with Near Eastern traditions.

title: Buddha at Bamiyan
materials: Cut rock with plaster and polychrome paint
description: Colossal statues carved into cliffs along the Silk Road, representing the universality of Buddha's teachings in a cosmopolitan style.

title: The Kaaba
materials: Granite masonry, covered with silk curtain
description: The center of the Islamic world and the destination of the Hajj. It is the focal point toward which all Muslims pray.

title: Jowo Rinpoche, enshrined in the Jokhang Temple
description: The most sacred statue in Tibet, believed to be an authentic likeness of the Buddha at age twelve.

title: Dome of the Rock
materials: Stone masonry, glazed ceramic tile, and gilt dome
description: A shrine marking the spot where Muhammad is believed to have ascended to heaven; its design is influenced by Byzantine architecture.

title: Great Mosque (Masjid-e Jameh)
materials: Stone, brick, and glazed ceramic tile
description: The prototype for the four-iwan mosque layout, which features a large open courtyard surrounded by four vaulted portals.

title: Folio from a Qur’an
artist: Arab, North Africa or Near East
date: c. eighth to ninth century C.E.
materials: Ink, color, and gold on parchment
description: Written in Kufic script, the horizontal format and bold calligraphy reflect the clarity and divine nature of the sacred text.

title: Basin (Baptistère de Saint Louis)
materials: Brass inlaid with gold and silver
description: An exquisite metalwork vessel used for the baptism of French royals, decorated with hunting scenes and courtly figures.

title: Bahram Gur Fights the Karg
materials: Ink and opaque watercolor, gold, and silver on paper
description: From a Persian epic, showing a hero conquering a mythical beast. It blends Persian narrative with Chinese-influenced landscapes.

title: The Court of Gayumars
materials: Ink, opaque watercolor, and gold on paper
description: A masterpiece of Safavid painting, illustrating the legendary first king of Iran and his court in a lush, detailed landscape.

title: The Ardabil Carpet
description: One of the largest and most intricate carpets in existence, created for a royal shrine and featuring a sunburst medallion.

title: Great Stupa at Sanchi
description: A monumental reliquary mound for the Buddha's remains. Pilgrims perform ritual circumambulation as a form of meditation.

title: Terra cotta warriors from mausoleum of the first Qin emperor of China
description: A massive army of individualized statues built to protect the first emperor in the afterlife, showing the scale of Qin state power.

title: Funeral banner of Lady Dai (Xin Zhui)
description: A T-shaped banner found in a noblewoman's tomb, illustrating the Han conception of the cosmos, from the underworld to heaven.

title: Longmen caves
description: A complex of thousands of Buddhist statues carved into river cliffs, centered on a massive Buddha figure and celestial attendants.

title: Gold and jade crown
description: Found in royal tombs, this crown features tree and antler-like projections, symbolizing the sacred power of the ruler and shamanistic connections to the spirit world.

title: Todai-ji
artist: Various artists, including Kei School sculptors
materials: Bronze and wood (sculpture); wood with ceramic-tile roofing (architecture)
description: The center of Buddhist authority in Japan, housing a colossal Great Buddha (Daibutsu) and guarded by fierce Nio statues.

title: Borobudur Temple
description: A massive three-dimensional mandala. Pilgrims circumambulate the terraces, symbolically moving from the world of desire to the world of formlessness.

title: Angkor, the temple of Angkor Wat, and the city of Angkor Thom
description: A vast complex serving as a Hindu and later Buddhist capital. The main temple is a physical representation of Mt. Meru, the cosmic home of the gods.

title: Lakshmana Temple
description: An ornate Hindu temple featuring a beehive-shaped tower (shikhara). Its exterior is covered in detailed sculptures of gods and erotic figures.

title: Travelers among Mountains and Streams
description: A quintessential monumental landscape painting. It reflects Neo-Confucian ideals, with tiny humans emphasizing the overwhelming scale of nature.

title: Shiva as Lord of Dance (Nataraja)
description: A portable icon depicting Shiva performing the cosmic dance of destruction and creation, balancing on the demon of ignorance.

title: Night Attack on the Sanjô Palace
description: An action-packed narrative scroll (emaki) that uses birds-eye perspective to document a brutal civil war event.

title: The David Vases
description: Highly influential examples of blue-and-white porcelain, featuring dragon and phoenix motifs and inscriptions detailing their offering to a temple.

title: Portrait of Sin Sukju
materials: Hanging scroll (ink and color on silk)
description: A meritorious portrait of a high-ranking official. It emphasizes Neo-Confucian values of ancestral worship and political loyalty.

title: Forbidden City
materials: Stone masonry, marble, brick, wood, and ceramic tile
description: The ritual and administrative heart of the Chinese empire, designed to reflect the cosmic order and the supreme authority of the Emperor.

title: Ryoan-ji
description: A Zen Buddhist dry garden (karesansui). Its 15 carefully placed stones encourage meditation on the nature of reality and emptiness.

title: Jahangir Preferring a Sufi Shaikh to Kings
materials: Watercolor, gold, and ink on paper
description: A Mughal miniature that asserts the Emperor's spiritual superiority over world leaders, including King James I of England.

title: Taj Mahal
artist: Masons, marble workers, mosaicists, and decorators working under Ustad Ahmad Lahori
materials: Stone masonry and marble with inlay of precious and semiprecious stones; gardens
description: A majestic mausoleum built by Shah Jahan for his wife. Its white marble and symmetrical gardens evoke a vision of Paradise.

title: White and Red Plum Blossoms
materials: Ink, watercolor, and gold leaf on paper
description: A masterpiece of the Rinpa school, using bold abstraction and flattened forms to create a highly decorative seasonal scene.

title: Under the Waves off Kanagawa (Kanagawa oki nami ura)
materials: Steel-blue woodblock print; ink and color on paper
description: Part of 'Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.' It captures the fleeting power of nature and became a world-renowned symbol of Japanese art.

title: Chairman Mao en Route to Anyuan
artist: Artist unknown; based on an oil painting by Liu Chunhua
description: A propaganda masterpiece using Socialist Realism to portray Mao Zedong as a heroic and visionary leader of the proletariat.

title: Nan Madol
description: An ancient megalithic city built on coral reefs. Its massive basalt structures served as the administrative and religious seat of the dynasty.

title: Moai on platform (ahu)
materials: Volcanic tuff figures on basalt base
description: Colossal stone statues representing deified ancestors. They face inland to watch over and protect the community.

title: 'Ahu 'ula (feather cape)
description: Worn by high-ranking chiefs, these capes required thousands of feathers from rare birds, symbolizing the wearer's divine protection and status.

title: Staff god
description: A rare surviving deity figure wrapped in barkcloth. It represents the generative power of the gods and ancestral lineages.

title: Female deity
description: A highly abstracted, minimalist figure carved from breadfruit wood. These statues were kept in cult houses to house specific deities.

title: Buk (mask)
materials: Turtle shell, wood, fiber, feathers, and shell
description: An intricate mask combining human and animal features (often a frigate bird), used in funerary ceremonies and rituals for good harvest.

title: Hiapo (tapa)
description: Niuean barkcloth known for its complex, fine-lined geometric patterns and botanical motifs, often used for clothing and ritual exchange.

title: Tamati Waka Nene
description: A posthumous portrait of a Maori chief. The painting records his prestige through his facial tattoos (moko) and status symbols like the greenstone earring.

title: Navigation chart
description: A mnemonic device (rebbelib) used by navigators to map ocean swell patterns and the location of islands using sticks and shells.

title: Malagan display and mask
artist: New Ireland Province, Papua New Guinea
description: Created for complex funerary cycles to honor the deceased. The masks are intricate and intended to be used only once before being destroyed or sold.

title: Presentation of Fijian mats and tapa cloths to Queen Elizabeth II
materials: Multimedia performance (costume; cosmetics, including scent; chant; movement; and pandanus fiber/hibiscus fiber mats), photographic documentation
description: A significant ritual event capturing the intersection of colonial history and indigenous Fijian cultural exchange.

title: The Gates
description: 7,503 saffron-colored fabric panels in Central Park, creating a 'golden river' that altered the viewer's experience of the urban landscape.

title: Vietnam Veterans Memorial
description: A minimalist, V-shaped wall inscribed with over 58,000 names. Its reflective surface integrates the viewer into the history of the fallen.

title: Horn Players
materials: Acrylic and oil paintstick on three canvas panels
description: A tribute to jazz legends Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie. Basquiat uses neo-expressionist style and wordplay to explore African American identity.

title: Summer Trees
description: Part of the 'Sumukwha' (literati ink painting) movement, using traditional materials to create a modern, abstract forest scene.

title: Androgyne III
materials: Burlap, resin, wood, nails, and string
description: A hollowed-out, backless figure that expresses the dehumanization and suffering of the 20th century while remaining gender-neutral.

title: A Book from the Sky
description: An installation of scrolls and books filled with thousands of meaningless 'Chinese' characters, questioning the reliability of language.

title: Pink Panther
description: Part of the 'Banality' series. Koons uses kitsch and pop culture imagery to challenge traditional notions of high art and good taste.

title: Untitled (#228), from the History Portraits series
description: Sherman poses as Judith holding the head of Holofernes, using costuming and prosthetics to deconstruct historical European painting styles.

title: Dancing at the Louvre, from the series The French Collection, Part I; #1
materials: Acrylic on canvas, bordering with fabric
description: A story quilt that rewrites art history by placing a fictional Black family in the Louvre, blending folk art and high art.

title: Trade (Gifts for Trading Land with White People)
materials: Oil and mixed media on canvas
description: A response to the Quincentenary of Columbus's arrival, using sports memorabilia and collage to critique the history of Native American exploitation.

title: Earth’s Creation
description: A massive, vibrant abstract work reflecting the 'green time' in the Australian desert after the rains, rooted in Aboriginal tradition.

title: Rebellious Silence, from the Women of Allah series
artist: Shirin Neshat (artist); photo by Cynthia Preston
description: Explores the intersection of femininity, religion, and violence in post-revolutionary Iran through the use of Persian calligraphy on a woman's face.

title: En la Barberia no se Llora (No Crying Allowed in the Barbershop)
description: A large-scale installation of a hyper-masculine barbershop that critiques machismo and Latino male identity.

title: Pisupo Lua Afe (Corned Beef 2000)
description: A life-sized bull sculpture made from recycled tin cans, commenting on the impact of global trade and processed foods on Pacific Island health.

title: Electronic Superhighway
materials: Mixed-media installation (47-channel closed-circuit video installation, neon, steel, and electronic components)
description: A massive neon-mapped US featuring screens that depict cultural stereotypes of each state, predicting our interconnected digital future.

title: The Crossing
description: A dual-screen video work where a man is slowly consumed by fire on one side and water on the other, exploring rebirth and destruction.

title: Guggenheim Museum Bilbao
description: A deconstructivist masterpiece known for its sweeping, metallic curves that revitalized the industrial city of Bilbao.

title: Pure Land
description: A futuristic depiction of the artist as a Buddhist deity, blending modern technology, video game aesthetics, and traditional spiritual themes.

title: Lying with the Wolf
description: Reimagining female figures from folklore, Smith depicts a woman calmly embracing a wolf to explore the link between humans and nature.

title: Darkytown Rebellion
materials: Cut paper and projection on wall
description: Using black paper silhouettes and colored light, Walker forces the viewer to confront the brutal and racist history of the American South.

title: The Swing (after Fragonard)
description: A headless mannequin in Dutch Wax fabrics recreates the Rococo painting, critiquing colonialism, wealth, and identity.

title: Old Man’s Cloth
materials: Aluminum and copper wire (recycled liquor bottle caps)
description: A tapestry-like sculpture made from discarded liquor labels, referencing the history of trade between Europe and Africa.

title: Stadia II
description: A multilayered abstract work suggesting a stadium atmosphere, exploring themes of nationalism, globalism, and public space.

title: Preying Mantra
description: A hybrid female figure in a lush landscape, questioning western stereotypes of the 'exotic' Black female body.

title: Shibboleth
description: A 548-foot crack in the museum floor that acts as a metaphor for the experience of exclusion, racism, and social borders.

title: MAXXI National Museum of XXI Century Arts
description: A fluid, futuristic building characterized by overlapping concrete volumes and rhythmic lines that guide visitors through space.

title: Kui Hua Zi (Sunflower Seeds)
description: 100 million hand-painted porcelain seeds exploring the 'Made in China' phenomenon and the relationship between the individual and the masses.
